content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 907 · SACRAMENTO, 95804 · · · 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance · · Administration · · Executive/legal · · Enforcement · · Statement of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662        322-3660           322-5901          322-6441            322-6464

August 26, 1980


Hugh R. Coffin
Newport Beach City Attorney
City Hall
3300 Newport Boulevard
Newport Beach, CA 92663

Dear Mr. Coffin:

This is in response to your letter of August 1, 1980,
requesting advice concerning the conflict of interest provisions
of the Political Reform Act on behalf of two Newport Beach
City Planning Commissioners, Allen Beek and Paul Ballalis.
The following advice is provided pursuant to Government Code
Section 83114(b).1/ The facts as I understand them from
your letter and from subsequent telephone conversations with
you and Bob Burnham of your office are as follows.

The Newport Beach Planning Commission is in the process
of holding hearings prior to making a recommendation to the
Newport Beach City Council on the adoption of a Local Coastal
Program (LCP) pursuant to the Coastal Act of 1976. The
policies thus far drafted by City staff for presentation to
the Planning Commission have all dealt with commercial development and uses in the Coastal Zone; those policies dealing
with residential development have not yet been drawn up by
staff and are not part of this request for advice. The LCP
will contain Development Policies which will be generally
applicable throughout the Coastal Zone in Newport Beach as
well as a Land Use Plan which will detail the application of
the development policies within distinct geographical areas
of the City.

Commissioner Allen Beek has a one-third interest in the
Balboa Island Ferry. Its landings and a marine railway for
ferry maintenance are located on tidelands which are held in
trust by the City and leased to the ferry. One terminal of

_________________________________

1/ All statutory references are to the Government Code
unless otherwise stated.

Hugh R. Coffin
August 26, 1980
Page Two


the ferry is located in Central Balboa, one of the areas
which are covered by that portion of the Land Use Plan of
the LCP which is now under consideration. In addition,
Mr. Beck has a one-third interest in another lease of tide-
lands held by the City which is in turn subleased to the
Irvine Company, which operates the Balboa Yacht Basin on
this property, and to individual homeowners in a residential
community called Beacon Bay. The master lease and the sub-
leases all expire in 1987 and the City has indicated it will
not extend or renew the master lease, although it is still
exploring the possibilities of releasing some or all of the
property to the present sublessees.

In addition to properties in Old Corona Del Mar, an
area which is not covered in the Land Use Plan of the LCP as
currently under consideration, Mr. Balalis currently owns or
has a part interest in property in three areas which are
covered: three residential buildings in Balboa Peninsula
Point, three residential properties in West Newport, and one
commercial property in McFadden Square Bayfront. A boatyard
in which Mr. Balalis does not have an investment is currently
being operated on the McFadden Square Bayfront property, and
Mr. Balalis and the other owners of this property applied
for and received a use permit allowing a proposed 5,900
square foot office building to exceed the height limit on
this property. This use permit expired today and the owners
took no further steps to construct the office building.
Within the last month, Mr. Balalis has sold a piece of com-
mercial property in the area referred to in the Land Use
Plan as McFadden Square.

Two of the proposed Development Policies would seem to
have particular application to the interests held by these
two Planning Commissioners. The first is the policy concerning
coastally dependent uses. The ferry in which Mr. Beck has
an interest, the Yacht Basin which is a subsidence on land
which is leased to Mr. Beck, and the boatyard situated on
Mr. Balalis's property would all be considered coastally
dependent uses. Second, the policy concerning public property
leaseholds could apply to the two tidelands leases in which
Mr. Beck has an interest - the one on which the ferry facilities
are located and the one which is subleased to the Irvine
Company and Beacon Bay homeowners.

Section 87100 of the Political Reform Act states that:

Hugh R. Coffin
August 26, 1980
Page Three


No public official at any level of state or local
government shall make, participate in making or in any
way attempt to use his official position to influence a
governmental decision in which he knows or has reason
to know he has a financial interest.

Section 87103 further provides that:

An official has a financial interest in a decision
within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on
the public generally, on:

(a) Any business entity in which the public official
has a direct or indirect interest worth more than one
thousand dollars ($1,000);

(b) Any real property in which the public official
has a direct or indirect interest worth more than one
thousand dollars ($1,000);

(c) Any source of income, . . . aggregating two
hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value provided
to, received by or promised to the public official within
twelve months prior to the time when the decision is
made; or

(d) Any business entity in which the public official
is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or
holds any position of management.

. . .

By regulation the Fair Political Practices Commission has
provided that the effect of a governmental decision is material
if it will have a significant effect on the business entity,
real property or source of income in question. 2 Cal. Adm.
Code Section 18702(a). Investments and interests in real
property of an official include a pro rata share of investments
and interests in real property of a business entity in which
the official, his spouse and dependent children collectively
or independently own 10 percent or more. See Sections 82029,
82033 and 82034. Income of an official includes a pro rata
share of income of a business entity in which the official
and his spouse own collectively or independently 10 percent
or more. See Section 82030.

Hugh R. Coffin
August 26, 1980
Page Four


     Land use planning policies may affect the value of real
property not only by limiting the permissible density of
development but also by limiting the uses to which the property
may be put.  This is undoubtedly true, for example, of the
policies in the draft LCP concerning coastally dependent
uses.  For example, the priority given by the LCP to coastally
dependent uses could easily make it much more difficult for
Mr. Balalis and the other owners of the McFadden Square
Bayfront property to build an office building on a site now
used by a coastally dependent boatyard.  This is also true
of the property in which Mr. Beck has an interest which is
now being used by the ferry.  Consequently, neither Mr. Balalis
nor Mr. Beck should participate as public officials in the
Planning Commission's deliberations concerning the policies
in the LCP relating to coastally dependent uses.

     In a like manner, the policies in the LCP concerning
public property leaseholds will have a contemplated direct
effect on such leaseholds.  As Mr. Beck holds an interest in
real property which consists of the public property leasehold
on which the ferry terminal and maintenance railroad operate,
he should not participate as a public official in Planning
Commission decisions concerning LCP policies on public property
leaseholds.  This conclusion is buttressed by the fact that
although his lease to the Balboa Yacht Basin/Beacon Bay
property does not constitute an "interest in real property"
under the Political Reform Act as it will expire in less
than ten years (see Section 62033), the subleases of that
property who pay rent of such amount that Mr. Beck's pro
rata share is more than $250 per year constitute a source of
income to him.  Given the City's plans to negotiate directly
with these sublessees, it is also likely that the LCP policies
concerning public property leaseholds will have a material
financial effect on these sources of income.

     The Land Use Plan which will be developed as part of
the LCP will establish the density and type of commercial
development and to some extent residential development in
relatively small areas of Newport Beach.  By addressing such
questions as the permissible amount of commercial development
and whether the commercial development should be oriented
towards residents or visitors, the Land Use Plan will not
only have obvious effects on the value of commercial property
but it will also affect traffic patterns, parking congestion,
and numerous other factors which determine the value of
residential property as well.  Therefore, public officials
should not participate in the application of the general

Hugh R. Coffin
August 26, 1980
Page Five

policies of the LCP to commercial development in the various
specific areas in which they own property. Although Mr. Balalis
no longer holds an interest in the real property in the
McFadden Square area, the purchasers of that commercial
property constitute a source of income to him, and he therefore
has a financial interest in a decision which will affect the
value of the property for twelve months after receiving the
proceeds of the sale. Consequently, Mr. Beek should not
participate in decisions concerning the Land Use Plan for
Central Balboa, and Mr. Balalis should not participate with
respect to Balboa Peninsula Point, West Newport, McFadden
Square or McFadden Square Bayfront.

Once Mr. Beek and Mr. Balalis have disqualified them-
selves from official participation in these matters by dis-
closing their financial interest in the decisions on the
Planning Commission's official public record, they may there-
after appear before the agency as members of the general
public to represent themselves on matters related to their
personal interests. See 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18700(d)(2)
and (f)(1). This exception will allow them to testify as
members of the general public with respect to policies and
plan elements which will not affect property in which they have
an interest. Assuming, however, that neither the income
Mr. Beek receives from the Balboa Yacht Basin/Beacon Bay
property nor the income Mr. Balalis received from the sale
of the McFadden Square property are somehow tied to the
value of that property, decisions relating to those properties
will not affect their personal interests, and the exception
does not therefore allow them to participate in those decisions
as members of the general public.

Before closing I note that certain facts raised in your
letter raise questions as to whether additional disqualification
may be required. Mr. Balalis has an investment in two banks
which are located in or adjacent to the Coastal Zone. You
have informed me that you do not think that he, his spouse
and children own more than 10 Percent of either bank. Never-
theless, if either bank concentrates its assets in commercial
property or if either bank has a large commercial development
pending which could be affected by the LCP, it may be that
the LCP would have a material financial effect on that bank.
If such were the case and if Mr. Balalis knew or had reason
to know of such facts, then additional disqualification
might be required. In addition, Mr. Balalis is a principal
in two real estate development companies that have from time-
to-time developed residential real property within the Coastal

Hugh R. Coffin
August 26, 1980
Page Six


Zone. These companies may well be affected by those portions
of the LCP dealing with residential development, but those
portions of the LCP are not yet before the Planning Commission.
If either company developed residential property in the
Coastal Zone on more than an occasional basis, however, it
could be that that company would also be affected by the
portions of the LCP relating to commercial development,
especially if the overall LCP favored commercial development
at the expense of residential development.

Finally, if an official is disqualified from participating
in certain particular policies or Land Use Plan areas of the
LCP, nothing prevents his participation in those policies or
areas in which he has no financial interest. In addition,
once the wording of the individual policies and Land Use
Plans for the individual areas have been worked out without
the official's participation in those areas in which he is
interested, and the Planning Commission is ready to take its
final vote on whether to recommend the entire LCP to the
City Council, the official can come back on the Commission
to cast his vote.

If you have any further questions, please do not hesitate
to contact me again.

Sincerely,

[signature]

Sarah T. Cameron
Deputy Chief for
Conflicts of Interest
Legal Division

STC:plh

August 1, 1980


Fair Political Practices Commission
Post Office Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804

     Attention: Dwight Dickerson

Gentlemen:

     The City of Newport Beach is requesting an opinion as to whether or not two members of the Newport Beach Planning Commission are permitted to participate in the public hearing process leading to the adoption of the Local Coastal Program (LCP) of the City of Newport Beach.

     The City of Newport Beach is presently in the process of adopting its LCP as mandated by the Coastal Act of 1976. The City has prepared a draft LCP, Land Use Plan and Development Policies, which will be the subject of a last two public hearings to be conducted by the Newport Beach Planning Commission, the first hearing to be held on August 7, 1980. It is anticipated that the Planning Commission will take "straw votes" with respect to many of the Land Use and Development Policies proposed by the draft LCP. These proposed Policies would be revised or deleted in accordance with these votes, the Commission would then take a vote as to the LCP as revised, and refer the approved draft to the City Council. The City Council will thereafter hold additional public hearings, after which the Council may further revise, and then adopt, the proposed LCP.

     The LCP embraces all areas within the City within the Coastal Zone. This includes approximately 38% of the land area of the City of Newport Beach and all the ocean and bay area located within the jurisdiction of the City of Newport Beach. Within the land area there are approximately 17,000 residential units which approximates 55% of the total residential units within the City.


City Hall • 3300 Newport Boulevard, Newport Beach, California 92663

Fair Political Practices Commission
August 1, 1980
Page Two


Two members of the Newport Beach Planning Commission own
property interests within the Coastal Zone subject to the LCP.
We are requesting your opinion as to whether or not
a conflict of interest exists, as defined in Sections 87100
and 87103 of the Government Code, such that one or both members of the Planning Commission would be precluded from participating in the public hearings at the Planning Commission
relating to the adoption of the LCP. Further, we are specifically interested in obtaining an opinion as to the ability
of these members to participate in the public hearings but
to abstain from participating in the deliberations and
decision-making process of the Commission. Finally, we further desire an opinion as to whether or not the Commissioners,
should they be deemed to have a financial interest in property subject to the LCP, could participate in the deliberations
and decision-making process of the Planning Commission on
those portions of the LCP in which they have no financial
interest. As a part of this final opinion request, it should
be pointed out that the LCP will be approved by the Planning
Commission with a single vote after its varied parts are considered and reviewed.

To enable an opinion to be rendered as quickly as possible,
we felt it important to include all Land Use Plan and Development Policies of the LCP that could relate to the interests of
the Planning Commissioners involved (see Exhibits A through C).
We will also discuss more thoroughly the business and property
interests of the Planning Commissioners.

The draft LCP contains 56 pages of background material, a Land
Use Plan, and Development Policies. Many of the policies are
substantially the same as policies mandated by the Coastal Act
of 1976. Other policies appear to be consistent with the
Coastal Act policies, but have been tailored to deal with situations unique to Newport Beach.

The Land Use Plan consists of text describing certain designated
geographic areas within the City, which areas have been historically identified as distinct, and generally commercial, areas
within the City of Newport Beach. In addition to the text, 67
pages of maps are included specifically identifying land uses

Fair Political Practices Commission
August 1, 1980
Page Three


within the Coastal Zone. The majority of the land area within
the Coastal Zone is presently designated in the Newport Beach
General Plan as residential, either single-family or multi-
family. There is no proposal in the Land Use Plan which
would change the residential land uses presently authorized
by the General Plan and zoning of the City of Newport Beach.
In the specific area descriptions where a business or property
interest of one of the Commissioners is located, we are includ-
ing the text of the area description as well as a copy of the
maps delineating the LCP Land Use Plan and a larger map loca-
ting the interest within the City of Newport Beach (Exhibit D).

We are listing below the specific property and business inter-
ests, each of which exceeds $1,000 in value, of the Planning
Commissioners involved to assist in the preparation of your
opinion. The facts we believe pertinent are as follows:

     A.  Commissioner Allen Beek

     1.  Commissioner Beek owns a one-third interest in
the Balboa Island ferry. The Balboa ferry operates between
Balboa Island and the Balboa Peninsula (Central Balboa). The
ferry has been in operation, in substantially the same fashion,
since 1919. The majority of the property utilized by the ferry
and support facilities is situated on private property, owned
by Mr. Beek's mother, which the Balboa ferry rents from her.
The ferry landings and a marine railway for ferry maintenance
are located on tidelands, held in trust by the City of Newport
Beach, and leased to the Balboa ferry. There is no similar
operation within the City of Newport Beach and, to our knowledge,
no similar operation has ever been proposed. The ferry is a
coastally dependent use and the Coastal Act, and the LCP, encourage, if not require, its continued operation.
Specific policies
under "Coastal Dependent Uses" and "Public Property Leaseholds"
would be applicable to this operation. Notwithstanding their
applicability, given the continuing financial viability of this
operation, the fact that the ferry is something of an institu-
tion, and the fact that demand for the services provided will
not be likely to be impacted, this office has difficulty in
discerning any reasonably foreseeable impact on this operation
given the adoption of any LCP.

Fair Political Practices Commission
August 1, 1980
Page Four


     2.  Commissioner Beek holds a one-third interest in
a master lease that covers a large segment of waterfront
property that includes the Balboa Yacht Basin and Beacon Bay.
The Balboa Yacht Basin consists of a marina with approximately
166 slips, a few small commercial facilities, and some multi-
family residential structures.  Beacon Bay consists of 72
single-family residences.  These developments are partially
situated on tidelands held in trust by the City of Newport
Beach.  In 1927 the City leased this property to J. A. Beek,
predecessor to Commissioner Beek.  The Balboa Yacht Basin was
subsequently subleased to The Irvine Company, the present day
operator.  Beacon Bay was, and is, subleased to the individual
homeowners.  All leases expire December 31, 1987.  The City is
presently investigating the possibility of releasing some of
all of this property to the present sublessees under terms and
conditions that would reflect the current market value of the
property.  It is clear that the City is not going to extent or
renew the master lease to Beek interests.  The policies set
forth under "Coastal Dependent Uses" and "Public Property
Leaseholds" are applicable to this property.

     B.  Commissioner Paul Balalis

          Commissioner Balalis owns certain parcels of developed
property in predominantly residential sections within the Newport
Beach Coastal Zone.  These holdings will be discussed below.  Mr.
Balalis is a licensed real estate broker but does not actively
participate in the real estate profession within the City of
Newport Beach.  Mr. Balalis is a principal in two real estate
development companies headquartered in the City of Newport Beach
and leases office space in Lido Village.  These companies have,
from time to time, developed real property within the City of
Newport Beach.  Commissioner Balalis owns stock in two financial
institutions having offices within the Coastal Zone in Newport
Beach.  One of the institutions, the Bank of Newport, has offices
in Cannery Village and two other offices on the fringe of the
Coastal Zone.  The other institution, Newport Harbour National
Bank, has a single office located outside, but adjacent to, the
Coastal Zone.  Mr. Balalis is a member of the Board of Directors
of Newport Harbour National Bank.  It is assumed that both banks
have a financial interest in property located within the Coastal
Zone in Newport Beach.  The draft LCP does not appear to contain
policies that directly affect either of the banks.

Fair Political Practices Commission
August 1, 1980
Page Five




     1.  Commissioner Balalis owns three residential
buildings in the Balboa Peninsula Point area.  These properties
are located between C and E Streets and front on Balboa Boulevard.
The existing public walkway, the extension of which is
proposed by the draft LCP, runs along the oceanfront side of
the buildings.  The residential character of this area has
remained relatively unchanged for many years.  The draft LCP
land use recommendations are in conformity with the present
makeup of the area.  The properties are located in area D, as
shown on the map which is attached as Exhibit "E."  As of
January 1, 1979, there were approximately 1,146 housing units
within this area.

     2.  Mr. Balalis owns three residential properties
situated in an area of the city known as Old Corona del Mar.
These properties are within the Coastal Zone.  These properties,
one of which is presently under construction, are situated within
areas F2 and F3 shown on Exhibit "E."  There are approximately
1,806 housing units within these areas.  There are no specific
policies within the draft LCP which directly impact these properties, nor are there any land use recommendations that reference
these areas.  The residential character of these areas has been
maintained, without significant change, for many years.

     3.  Commissioner Balalis owns three residential properties in the West Newport area.  These properties are situated
within areas B1 and B2 as shown on Exhibit "E."  As of January 1,
1979, there were approximately 1,397 residential units within
these areas.  The draft LCP recommends no change in the land use
designations for the residential areas of West Newport.  Two of
the properties are outside the area served by the present walkway,
but it is unlikely that the extension of the walkway would have
any bearing on the value of the property.  The recommended land
use designations for the existing retail-commercial properties
that border Pacific Coast Highway in West Newport are in conformance with the existing uses.

     4.  Commissioner Balalis has an interest in property
located at 2005 West Balboa Boulevard.  This property is presently
in escrow and escrow is scheduled to close August 15, 1980.  This
property is located within area B5 on Exhibit "E" and is within

Fair Political Practices Commission
August 1, 1980
Page Six




the area designated by the LCP as McFadden Square. The draft
LCP land use recommendation is Recreational/Marine Commercial.
The purpose of such a designation is, according to the LCP,
"to maintain the existing orientation while allowing an up-
grading of the area." Office uses within such an area would
be limited to space above the first floor and only in cases
where the ground level is occupied by a permitted use. This
is an area that is presently undergoing a certain amount of
change, physically in the form of rehabilitation of existing
structures.

     5. Mr. Balalis presently has an interest in property
located at 227 - 20th Street, Newport Beach. This property
is within McFadden Square Bayfront, as designated by the draft
LCP, and within area B5 as shown on Exhibit "F." The draft
LCP land use recommendation for this area is Recreational and
Marine Commercial and Retail and Service Commercial. This
recommended use is consistent with existing uses. This prop-
erty is presently being utilized as a boat paint yard, a use
which would fall within the description of "Coastal Dependent Uses."
The business is owned and operated by joint property owners.

This office has reviewed opinions rendered by the FPPC in
matters bearing some resemblance to the facts of this case.
In the Matter of Opinion Requested by Ogelsby, 1 FPPC 71;
In the Matter of Opinion Requested by Biondo, 1 FPPC 77;
In the Matter of Opinion Requested by Owen, 2 FPPC 7;
In the Matter of Opinion Requested by Hopkins, 3 FPPC 107; and
In the Matter of Opinion Requested by Brown, 4 FPPC 19. Our
review of the draft LCP and the property interests discussed
above leads us to the conclusion that, at least with respect to
the residential property interests, there is no reasonably fore-
seeable material financial effect, distinguishable from the public
generally, that would flow from the adoption of the LCP. The only
effect that we could contrive would be the continuing desirability
of these residential areas through a program that preserves those
uses that gave the City its charm and ambience. This benefit
would accrue to all residential property owners and fall within
the holdings of Owen, supra.

Fair Political Practices Commission
August 1, 1980
Page Seven




While the question is somewhat closer, our opinion is the
same where commercial properties are concerned. Although
certain policies are directly applicable to these holdings,
those policies do not require nor recommend, except in very
limited instances, affirmative conduct on the part of the
City that would change the status quo. The policies that
relate to coastally dependent uses and visitor serving facili-
ties are general and often contradict one another (e.g. en-
courage such uses but limit the ability to construct them in
light of environmental and public access concerns) such that
their potential effect is difficult or impossible to predict.
The possibility that adoption of the LCP would tend to pre-
serve existing marine uses, thus restricting competition, is
offset by the fact that owners of property that house such
uses are restricted from developing their property in a more
profitable manner. The analysis of the impact of the LCP on
commercial properties is similar to that undertaken in
inquiry "C" in Owen, supra, and the result should be similar.

We realize that you will receive this material shortly before
the first scheduled Planning Commission public hearing, and we
ask that you advise us by phone, if possible as to your
thoughts with respect to the participation of the two commis-
sioners in the hearings thereafter abstaining from delibera-
tions and/or voting on issues which may relate to their holdings.

Very truly yours,

[signature]
HUGH R. COFFIN
City Attorney

City of
Newport Beach

[Legend showing three diagonal line patterns]
Statutory dislocations
Statutory areas
Newport Beach

Prepared by McKinney Planning

EXH/13/IT E

sections.question: The City of Newport Beach is requesting an opinion as to whether or not two members of the Newport Beach Planning Commission are permitted to participate in the public hearing process leading to the adoption of the Local Coastal Program (LCP) of the City of Newport Beach. Specifically: (1) whether a conflict of interest exists under Sections 87100 and 87103 of the Government Code such that one or both members would be precluded from participating in the public hearings; (2) whether these members can participate in public hearings but abstain from deliberations and decision-making; and (3) whether Commissioners with financial interests in property subject to the LCP could participate in deliberations on portions of the LCP in which they have no financial interest, given that the LCP will be approved by a single vote.
sections.question_synthetic: Do two Planning Commission members with property interests in the Coastal Zone subject to the proposed Local Coastal Program have a disqualifying conflict of interest under Government Code Sections 87100 and 87103, and if so, what forms of limited participation are permitted?
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter from City of Newport Beach to FPPC, not an FPPC response. No conclusion section present. Document contains detailed factual background about two commissioners (Allen Beek and Paul Balalis) and their respective property/business interests. Text is partially corrupted at beginning but main request letter is legible. Document appears to be page 1-6 of a longer submission with exhibits.

Specifically: (1) whether a conflict of interest exists under Sections 87100 and 87103 of the Government Code such that one or both members would be precluded from participating in the public hearings; (2) whether these members can participate in public hearings but abstain from deliberations and decision-making; and (3) whether Commissioners with financial interests in property subject to the LCP could participate in deliberations on portions of the LCP in which they have no financial interest, given that the LCP will be approved by a single vote.

embedding.summary: The City of Newport Beach requests FPPC advice on whether two Planning Commission members with property interests in the Coastal Zone can participate in public hearings and deliberations regarding adoption of the Local Coastal Program, citing specific property holdings and business interests of each commissioner.
